definition: Ingenuous means innocent, candid, and unguarded in a way that suggests inexperience. Ingenious, its look-alike, means cleverly original and inventive, the mark of a brilliant idea or design. The words look almost identical but mean nearly opposite things.
commonMisuse: One transposed pair of letters separates them, so writers praise a clever fix as "ingenuous" or describe a naive remark as "ingenious." Worse, ingenuous gets tangled with disingenuous, its own opposite, which can flip a compliment into an insult.
exampleCorrect: The young engineer's ingenious fix for the leak impressed everyone, though her ingenuous surprise at the applause was just as charming.
exampleMisused: The engineer came up with an ingenuous fix for the leak using a coat hanger and a strip of duct tape.

definition: Marked by a meaningful contrast between expectation and reality; characterized by a result that pointedly undercuts what was intended or anticipated.
commonMisuse: Frequently used to mean any odd coincidence rather than a true reversal of expectation. A rainy wedding is unlucky; a meteorologist's wedding rained out by a storm she failed to predict is ironic. MW notes the loose use has been around for a century, but the sharper sense is what carries the wit.
exampleCorrect: There is something deeply ironic about a fire chief whose own house burned down because the smoke detector battery had died.
exampleMisused: It's so ironic, I was just thinking about my cousin when she called me out of the blue this morning.

definition: A nonstandard synonym for regardless. The ir- prefix is a redundant negative tacked onto a word that already means without regard, producing a kind of double negative.
commonMisuse: People use irregardless to sound emphatic, but most readers and listeners hear it as an error. It has been in use for over a century and appears in dictionaries, yet it remains widely disliked. Regardless does the same work without the wince.
exampleCorrect: She planned to climb the ridge that weekend regardless of the forecast, having waited two years for the permit.
exampleMisused: He insisted he would attend the meeting irregardless of what his doctor had told him about rest.

definition: Judicious means showing good judgment — wise, prudent, sensible. Judicial, its near-twin, means relating to courts, judges, or the administration of justice.
commonMisuse: People reach for the grander-sounding 'judicial' when they mean 'judicious,' as in 'a judicial use of the budget.' Both trace back to the Latin for judge, but only judicious means wise; judicial belongs to the courtroom.
exampleCorrect: Her judicious budget cuts spared the essentials — a restraint the judicial branch rarely manages with its own funding.
exampleMisused: He praised the committee's judicial spending, when he simply meant they had been wise with the money.

definition: The punishment or reward that someone genuinely deserves. Here "deserts" is an old noun meaning "things deserved," related to "deserve" and stressed on the second syllable, like the verb.
commonMisuse: Almost everyone writes "just desserts," imagining a fitting comeuppance served up like a final course. The pronunciation matches the sweet "dessert," and the food metaphor is so satisfying that the historically correct spelling looks like a typo.
exampleCorrect: After years of cheating customers, the contractor lost his license and his reputation, finally getting his just deserts.
exampleMisused: The con artist got caught at last and received his just desserts.

definition: Having a nightmarish, disorienting quality marked by senseless complexity and oppressive bureaucracy, in which individuals are trapped by illogical systems they cannot understand or escape.
commonMisuse: It is often used loosely for anything merely annoying or inconvenient. Properly, Kafkaesque, after the writer Franz Kafka, describes a specifically surreal, bureaucratic absurdity that leaves a person helpless and bewildered.
exampleCorrect: Renewing the permit became Kafkaesque: each office insisted he first obtain a form only that same office could issue.
exampleMisused: He found the long coffee-shop line this morning genuinely Kafkaesque.

definition: Lay means to put or place something down and is transitive, requiring an object: you lay a book on the table. Lie means to recline and is intransitive, taking no object: you lie on the couch.
commonMisuse: The pair tangles because lay is also the past tense of lie, so "I lay down yesterday" is correct while "I'm going to lay down now" is not. In the present tense, use lie when nothing is being placed and lay only when there's an object.
exampleCorrect: After she laid the quilt over the daybed, she went to lie down on the screened porch and listen to the cicadas.
exampleMisused: The cat likes to lay in the patch of sun by the kitchen window every afternoon.

definition: Libel and slander are both forms of defamation, a false statement that damages a reputation. Libel is the written or published form; slander is the spoken form. The medium is the whole distinction.
commonMisuse: People use either word for any insult, treating them as synonyms, because both name reputational harm. But the legal line holds: a damaging magazine article or tweet is potentially libel, while a damaging remark at a dinner party is slander.
exampleCorrect: The magazine feared the printed claim was libel, while the editor's offhand remark at the gala edged toward slander.
exampleMisused: She threatened to sue the newspaper for slander after the article appeared on the front page.

definition: In a strictly factual, non-figurative sense; word for word, exactly as described. Also widely used as a hyperbolic intensifier meaning 'virtually' or 'figuratively.'
commonMisuse: Used as a figurative intensifier, as in 'I literally died,' which is the opposite of the strict sense. MW notes this hyperbolic use goes back to 1769 and has appeared in dictionaries since 1909, so it is hardly new. Strict writers still reserve literally for the precise sense to avoid pulling the rug out from under it.
exampleCorrect: The translator stuck literally to the original, preserving even the awkward word order of the German source.
exampleMisused: By the end of the hike, my feet were literally on fire, and I had to soak them in the creek before I could walk back to the car.

definition: An exclamation expressing surprise at something noticed or revealed. 'Lo' is an old interjection meaning 'look,' and the phrase has the flavor of a storyteller pointing dramatically at the moment of discovery.
commonMisuse: Lo and behold sometimes gets written as 'low and behold,' because 'lo' is rare outside this phrase and a few biblical contexts. There is nothing low about the expression — 'lo' is simply an archaic command to look. Pair it with 'behold' (also meaning to look) and the doubling makes sense.
exampleCorrect: I rummaged through the attic for hours and, lo and behold, found my grandmother's wedding ring inside an old cigar box.
exampleMisused: Low and behold, the missing dog turned up on the neighbor's porch, the newsletter read, instead of lo and behold.

definition: Loath, without a final e, is an adjective meaning reluctant or unwilling, usually paired with to: loath to admit. Loathe, with the e, is a verb meaning to hate intensely or feel deep disgust.
commonMisuse: The two differ by one silent letter, so writers add the e to the adjective ("loathe to apologize") or drop it from the verb ("she loaths liver"). If you mean reluctance, use loath; if you mean hatred, use loathe.
exampleCorrect: She was loath to attend the reunion because she loathed the forced small talk that always filled the first hour.
exampleMisused: The senator seemed loathe to take questions, ducking out the side door before reporters could catch him.

definition: Characterized by cunning, calculated deceit, and a willingness to use unscrupulous methods, especially in pursuit of political power or personal advantage.
commonMisuse: It is often used loosely for any sneakiness or mild trickery. Strictly, Machiavellian, after the political writer Niccolo Machiavelli, implies a deliberate, strategic ruthlessness in which ends justify the means.
exampleCorrect: The senator's Machiavellian maneuvering pitted his rivals against each other until he alone remained for the nomination.
exampleMisused: She called her coworker Machiavellian for quietly taking the last good parking spot.

definition: To get by with whatever resources happen to be available, even if they fall short of ideal. You make do with leftovers, with a borrowed tool, with whatever the situation hands you.
commonMisuse: Make do is often written as 'make due,' as though something were being made to come due, like a bill. The original is plainer: to make something do — that is, to make it suffice. 'Due' suggests obligation or arrival, which has nothing to do with the meaning.
exampleCorrect: When the power cut out, the diner had to make do with candles and a propane grill until the lights came back at midnight.
exampleMisused: We'll just have to make due with the smaller conference room this week, the calendar invite said, when make do was the correct phrase.

definition: A medium is a single means of communication or expression, such as television or oil paint. Media is the plural, and is also used collectively for the press and broadcasting.
commonMisuse: People write 'a media' or 'social media is a powerful media' when they mean one channel. Because 'media' dominates everyday speech, the singular 'medium' feels artistic or technical, so the plural gets pressed into singular service.
exampleCorrect: Radio remains a surprisingly intimate medium, even as audiences scatter across newer media like podcasts and streaming video.
exampleMisused: Instagram has become the most influential media for young consumers.

definition: Mettle is a person's courage, spirit, and resilience under pressure. Metal, its homophone, is a hard, typically shiny material like iron or gold.
commonMisuse: The homophones share an origin, which makes the slip feel almost reasonable. People write 'test his metal' for proving someone's character. The phrase is 'test his mettle': mettle, with the double T, is the inner grit, not the substance.
exampleCorrect: The grueling final exam tested every student's mettle, while the chemistry section drilled them on which metal conducts heat fastest.
exampleMisused: The championship game was a true test of the young team's metal.

definition: Militate means to have force or weight as a factor working against something, almost always paired with against. Mitigate means to make something less severe, harmful, or intense. The two pull in opposite directions.
commonMisuse: They sound alike, so writers blend them into "mitigate against," or use militate where they mean to lessen. Heavy rain militates against the picnic; an umbrella mitigates the soaking. Use mitigate alone, or militate strictly with against.
exampleCorrect: Rising interest rates militate against the bank's expansion, though new reserves should mitigate the damage if the deal still proceeds.
exampleMisused: The new safety protocols are designed to militate the risk of accidents on the assembly line.

definition: To soften what you say with careful, polite phrasing. The idiom almost always appears in the negative, describing someone who speaks bluntly and refuses to dress up a hard truth.
commonMisuse: Mince words is so commonly used in the negative ('she doesn't mince words') that some writers reach for it positively, as in 'he minced words for the sake of the audience.' That works grammatically but feels off; the natural home of the phrase is the negative form, where the contrast with bluntness lives.
exampleCorrect: The coach doesn't mince words after a loss — players know to expect a frank breakdown of every missed assignment.
exampleMisused: She minced her words carefully during the eulogy, the article said, where 'chose her words carefully' would have been more natural.

definition: To be careful about your behavior, especially to be polite or well-mannered. The origin is disputed, with theories ranging from printers' typesetting to barkeepers tracking pints and quarts.
commonMisuse: Nobody fully agrees what the p's and q's originally stood for, which leads to confident but conflicting explanations. The phrase itself is stable; the etymology isn't. Use it for manners and don't get pinned down on which trade invented it.
exampleCorrect: She told the children to mind their p's and q's at their great-aunt's house, especially around the porcelain figurines.
exampleMisused: He told the interns to mind their p's and q's, then explained at length an etymology he had invented on the spot.

definition: Extremely small, tiny, or negligible in size or amount. It derives from the Latin 'minus', meaning less.
commonMisuse: People spell it 'miniscule', assuming it relates to 'mini'. It does not; the root is 'minus'. The 'mini' spelling is so widespread it is now sometimes listed as a variant, but careful writers and editors still treat it as an error.
exampleCorrect: The pay raise turned out to be minuscule, barely enough to cover the rising cost of her morning coffee.
exampleMisused: There was only a miniscule chance the flight would leave on time.

definition: Inclined to playful trouble-making; causing minor harm or annoyance in a teasing, often endearing way. It has three syllables, stressed on the first: MIS-chuh-vus.
commonMisuse: Many people say and spell it "mischievious," adding a fourth syllable and stressing the second: mis-CHEE-vee-us. The extra "i" mirrors common words like "devious" and "previous," so the pattern feels natural even though it is wrong.
exampleCorrect: The puppy wore a mischievous expression, the kind that always meant a shoe had already been hidden somewhere in the house.
exampleMisused: He gave her a mischievious grin before revealing the surprise party.

definition: Carrying two related senses. One: open to argument or debate, as in a moot point worth discussing. Two: stripped of practical relevance, as in a question already settled by events.
commonMisuse: The two senses sit awkwardly together. Speakers often use moot to mean not worth discussing, which is closer to the second sense than the first. Both meanings are standard, so context usually has to do the work of telling them apart.
exampleCorrect: Whether the bill would have passed became moot the moment the governor announced she was withdrawing it.
exampleMisused: He kept arguing about the route, but with the road closed, the whole debate was a moot point for discussion.

definition: Morale is a noun naming the collective mood, confidence, and enthusiasm of a group. Moral, its near-twin, is an adjective relating to principles of right and wrong, or a noun meaning the lesson of a story.
commonMisuse: Writers drop the final e and write "moral" when they mean group spirit, turning a slump in team enthusiasm into an ethical failing. One trick: the e in morale stands for emotion, the feeling that runs through a team.
exampleCorrect: She made the moral choice to report the fraud, even knowing the disclosure would shatter morale across her own department.
exampleMisused: After the layoffs, the moral on the engineering team dropped to its lowest point in years.

definition: Traditionally, something nauseous is what causes sickness, while to be nauseated is to feel sick to your stomach. A nauseous smell leaves you nauseated.
commonMisuse: Almost everyone now says 'I feel nauseous' to mean they feel ill, and Merriam-Webster accepts it. But careful writers keep the older split: nauseous causes the sickness, nauseated is the sickness felt.
exampleCorrect: The nauseous reek from the bilge left half the passengers nauseated before the ferry had even cleared the harbor.
exampleMisused: After the roller coaster she said she felt nauseous, when traditionally she was the one nauseated.

definition: An agent of inescapable retribution or downfall; also a long-standing rival or opponent so formidable as to seem destined to defeat or frustrate someone.
commonMisuse: It is often watered down to mean any rival or competitor. Originally the Greek goddess of retributive justice, nemesis properly carries a sense of fated, deserved comeuppance, not mere rivalry.
exampleCorrect: Years of fraud finally caught up with the financier when a dogged prosecutor became his nemesis.
exampleMisused: She jokingly called the coworker who beat her at office trivia her nemesis.

definition: Causing intense stress, anxiety, or tension. The image is of one's nerves being stretched painfully on the rack, the same torture device behind "rack one's brain."
commonMisuse: "Nerve-wracking" is extremely common and now widely accepted, but the original and still-preferred spelling is "nerve-racking." Writers choose "wrack" because frayed nerves feel like wreckage, and the variant has become so frequent it looks correct.
exampleCorrect: Waiting for the biopsy results over a long holiday weekend was the most nerve-racking stretch of her year.
exampleMisused: The final round of interviews was absolutely nerve-wracking.

definition: To stop something at an early stage, before it has a chance to develop into a real problem. The image is from gardening: snipping a bud off a plant prevents it from blooming.
commonMisuse: The phrase is sometimes heard as 'nip it in the butt,' which switches a horticultural image for an anatomical one. The point of the original is timing — catching a problem before it flowers. A nip in the bud is preventive; a nip in the butt is just bewildering.
exampleCorrect: The principal wanted to nip the cheating in the bud before midterms, so she gave the same warning in every homeroom.
exampleMisused: We need to nip this gossip in the butt before HR gets involved, the manager wrote, instead of nip it in the bud.

definition: Offensive or disgusting, especially because of a foul smell; broadly, harmful or unpleasant in a sickening way.
commonMisuse: Reliably mistaken for noisy because of the spelling, but the two words are unrelated. Noisome traces back to annoy, not noise, and almost always describes something that stinks or sickens, not something loud.
exampleCorrect: A noisome cloud rolled off the rendering plant whenever the wind shifted, making the whole neighborhood reach for the windows.
exampleMisused: The noisome rehearsal next door went on past midnight, and we could feel the bass through our kitchen floor.

definition: From French non pareil, literally not equal. It describes someone or something that has no peer or rival; in its strict sense, the absolute best of its kind, not merely excellent.
commonMisuse: Nonpareil gets used loosely to mean very good, but the word carries a stronger claim than that. Strictly, calling a chef a nonpareil means no other chef alive matches them, not simply that they're talented.
exampleCorrect: Among twentieth-century cellists, Casals was a nonpareil, his Bach recordings setting a standard no contemporary equaled.
exampleMisused: The bakery on the corner makes a nonpareil croissant, easily one of the better ones in the neighborhood.

definition: So thoroughly surprised, confused, or thrown off that one is briefly unable to think clearly or decide how to react.
commonMisuse: Frequently used to mean unfazed or unbothered, which is the opposite. The non- looks like a negating prefix, which fuels the confusion. MW notes the 'unfazed' sense as a chiefly American development, but most editors still treat it as an error.
exampleCorrect: The young clerk stood nonplussed at the counter, holding a passport she had no idea how to process under the new rules.
exampleMisused: He seemed totally nonplussed by the boos from the crowd and just kept playing the same chord progression.

definition: Unremarkable; not impressive or noteworthy enough to mention with any enthusiasm. The image is of a traveler so unmoved by an experience that it would not earn a line in a letter back home.
commonMisuse: Some people say "nothing to ride home about," picturing a triumphant return on horseback. The swap feels plausible because the phrase already evokes a journey, and "ride" and "write" sound similar in fast speech, so the homecoming image quietly drifts from the mailbox to the saddle.
exampleCorrect: The hotel had a great view, but the breakfast buffet was lukewarm coffee and stale pastries, really nothing to write home about.
exampleMisused: The concert was fine, but honestly the opening act was nothing to ride home about.

definition: Relating to the nucleus of an atom or a cell, or to the energy released by atomic reactions. It is properly pronounced NOO-klee-er, in two clear back-half syllables.
commonMisuse: Many speakers, including some prominent public figures, say "nucular" (NOO-kyuh-ler). The brain reshapes the unfamiliar "-clear" ending into the more common "-cular" pattern found in words like "molecular" and "particular."
exampleCorrect: The plant's engineers explained how the nuclear reactor's cooling system would shut down safely in an emergency.
exampleMisused: He spent the whole briefing talking about nucular power without anyone correcting him.

definition: To make something unnecessary by anticipating and removing the need for it; to prevent a difficulty from arising in the first place.
commonMisuse: Mistaken for 'make obvious' because of the spelling, but the two words only share Latin roots. To obviate a problem is to head it off, not to point it out. The right paraphrase is almost always 'render unnecessary' or 'preclude.'
exampleCorrect: Installing the backup generator obviated the need to evacuate the data center every time a summer storm rolled through.
exampleMisused: Her tone obviated the fact that she had been dreading the family dinner all week.

definition: Something that happens; an event, incident, or instance of something taking place. It is the noun form of the verb "occur."
commonMisuse: People write "occurance," using the more common "-ance" ending and a single r. The correct form doubles the r and ends in "-ence," but since the ending is unstressed and "-ance" words are everywhere, the wrong spelling sounds just as right.
exampleCorrect: A black bear wandering through the suburb's main street was a rare occurrence that drew a crowd of phone-wielding onlookers.
exampleMisused: Power outages have become a regular occurance in our neighborhood this winter.

definition: Officious means meddlesome and self-important, the trait of someone who pushes unwanted help and takes charge uninvited. Official, its look-alike, means belonging to or authorized by an office or governing body; formally sanctioned.
commonMisuse: Officious sounds like a fancier official, so writers use it as a compliment when it is actually an insult. An official letter comes from someone in authority; an officious letter comes from someone who appointed themselves to write it.
exampleCorrect: The mayor's office released an official statement, but it was an officious clerk who insisted on reading it aloud twice to the press.
exampleMisused: She showed up at the courthouse with an officious document signed by the deputy clerk that morning.

definition: In a state of nervous suspense, waiting tensely for something to happen. Tenterhooks were the iron hooks that stretched wet cloth taut on a frame called a tenter as it dried.
commonMisuse: On tenterhooks is often written as 'on tenderhooks,' likely because tender feels closer to the tender emotional state the phrase describes. But the hooks in question are not soft. They held cloth under tension on a drying frame, which is exactly the suspended, stretched feeling the idiom captures.
exampleCorrect: The whole jury sat on tenterhooks as the foreman unfolded the verdict, his hands visibly trembling.
exampleMisused: We were on tenderhooks waiting for the adoption agency to call, she posted, where tenterhooks was the proper word.

definition: In flight from pursuers, especially fleeing the police or escaping captivity. "Lam" is old slang meaning to run off quickly, related to an archaic sense of "lam" meaning to beat or strike.
commonMisuse: People write "on the lamb," picturing a baby sheep. "Lam" is obscure slang that means nothing to most people, while "lamb" is a familiar word that sounds identical, so the brain supplies the animal it already knows.
exampleCorrect: The embezzler spent two years on the lam in three countries before a tip led marshals to a beach town in Costa Rica.
exampleMisused: After the heist, the robbers were on the lamb for months.

definition: A single thing rather than two; identical. The phrase emphasizes that two names, descriptions, or apparent entities actually refer to the very same person or thing.
commonMisuse: The phrase is often heard and written as 'one in the same,' which scans well but doesn't quite parse. The original means literally one — the same — pressing both ideas together. 'One in the same' suggests something nested inside itself, which is not the picture the idiom intends.
exampleCorrect: It turned out the anonymous donor and the museum's harshest critic were one and the same retired professor.
exampleMisused: The CEO and the chairman are one in the same person, the bio claimed, instead of one and the same.

definition: All at once, in a single swift and often devastating action. "Fell" is an archaic adjective meaning fierce, cruel, or deadly, related to the word "felon."
commonMisuse: People say "one foul swoop," hearing "foul" because the phrase describes something destructive and "fell" no longer means anything to modern ears. The bird-of-prey imagery makes "swoop" feel menacing, so a familiar negative word slides in.
exampleCorrect: The merger eliminated three regional offices in one fell swoop, and two hundred employees learned their fate by email.
exampleMisused: The storm knocked out power to the whole valley in one foul swoop.

definition: Ordnance is military hardware: weapons, ammunition, and the equipment used to maintain them. An ordinance, its near-twin, is a local law or formal decree, usually passed by a city or town council. A single letter i separates them.
commonMisuse: The words are near-homophones differing only by that missing i, so writers drift between them. But one is a parking rule and the other is artillery shells. Unexploded ordnance is a buried bomb; an unexploded ordinance is just a law nobody enforces.
exampleCorrect: The city ordinance banned fireworks downtown, even as Navy crews cleared old ordnance from the harbor a mile away.
exampleMisused: Crews spent the week clearing unexploded ordinance left behind from a training exercise in the 1950s.

definition: Suggestive of the authoritarian world of George Orwell's 1984, marked by pervasive surveillance, manipulative propaganda, and the systematic control of information and thought.
commonMisuse: It is often used loosely to mean simply creepy, unpleasant, or strict. Properly, Orwellian refers to the specific apparatus of authoritarian state control, surveillance, and distortion of truth.
exampleCorrect: The regime's Orwellian ministry rewrote old newspapers nightly so the leader's every prediction appeared correct.
exampleMisused: She thought the new motion-sensor lights in the hallway were a bit Orwellian.

definition: Palate is the roof of the mouth, or one's sense of taste. Palette is the board a painter mixes colors on, or a range of colors. Pallet is a flat wooden platform for moving cargo, or a thin straw mattress.
commonMisuse: All three are pronounced alike, so writers grab whichever spelling comes first, praising a chef's "palette" or a mural's "pallet." Keep them sorted by setting: a wine palate, a paint palette, a warehouse pallet.
exampleCorrect: The chef trusted her palate, the painter loaded her palette, and the dockworker stacked another pallet by the loading bay.
exampleMisused: The chef praised her young apprentice's palette for spice and her instinct for balance.

definition: A source of many serious and unforeseen troubles that, once set loose, cannot be contained again, so that opening it triggers irreversible and spreading harm.
commonMisuse: It is often used merely for a sensitive or awkward topic. In the myth, Pandora releases all the world's evils irreversibly, so the term properly stresses that the troubles, once unleashed, cannot be undone.
exampleCorrect: Investigating one suspicious invoice opened a Pandora's box of fraud the auditors could never fully close.
exampleMisused: Bringing up vacation plans at dinner opened a Pandora's box of mild disagreement we resolved by dessert.

definition: A model, pattern, or example; in science, an entire framework of theory, methods, and assumptions that shapes how a field understands and investigates its subject.
commonMisuse: Since Thomas Kuhn popularized 'paradigm shift,' the word is often used loosely as a buzzword for any change or new idea. Properly, a paradigm is a comprehensive underlying framework, not merely a fresh approach.
exampleCorrect: The shift from Newtonian physics to relativity represented a genuine change of scientific paradigm in the early twentieth century.
exampleMisused: The marketing team announced their new logo as a bold paradigm shift for the brand.

definition: A statement or situation that seems self-contradictory or impossible but may, on closer inspection, turn out to be true or at least meaningful. The contradiction is part of the point.
commonMisuse: Paradox often gets stretched to mean simply ironic or surprising. Strictly, a paradox involves an apparent contradiction. Calling it a paradox that the quiet kid grew up to be a comedian is a stretch; that's just unexpected. A real paradox tangles you up when you try to think about it.
exampleCorrect: Zeno's paradox argues that motion is impossible because crossing any distance requires first crossing half of it, then half of that, forever.
exampleMisused: He called it a paradox that the cheapest item in the store was also the most popular, when really it was just predictable.

definition: To be inspected and judged acceptable; to meet the required standard. A "muster" is a formal gathering of troops for review, so passing it means surviving scrutiny.
commonMisuse: People say "pass mustard," hearing the familiar condiment instead of the military term "muster." The two sound nearly identical, and since "muster" is uncommon, the brain reaches for the everyday word even though it makes no sense.
exampleCorrect: The contractor's quick patch job did not pass muster with the building inspector, who flagged six code violations.
exampleMisused: I rewrote the essay twice, but it still didn't pass mustard with my professor.

definition: Pique, as a verb, means to stir up or excite, especially curiosity or interest. Its two homophones do other jobs: peak is the pointed top or highest point of something, and peek is a quick or secretive look. Three homophones, three jobs.
commonMisuse: Because all three sound identical, writers reach for the most familiar spelling, producing "peak my interest" or "peak through the blinds." The correct phrase is "pique my interest"; a trailer can't peak your curiosity unless your curiosity is a mountain.
exampleCorrect: From the mountain peak she stopped to peek through her binoculars, and a flash of movement piqued her curiosity at once.
exampleMisused: The opening scene was strong enough to peak my interest, even though I usually avoid horror films.

definition: To peddle is to sell goods, often door to door. Its two homophones: a pedal is a foot-operated lever (and to pedal is to work one), and a petal is one of the colored segments of a flower.
commonMisuse: All three sound alike, so the wrong one slips in by ear. 'Peddle' is the trickiest: people write 'pedal his wares' for selling. Remember peddle and sell both have a double letter, and a peddler pushes products, not bicycle levers.
exampleCorrect: He would pedal through the park each morning, past vendors trying to peddle flowers whose petals had already begun to wilt.
exampleMisused: The street vendor tried to pedal knockoff watches to passing tourists.

definition: Next to last; second from the end of a series, never beyond the end.
commonMisuse: Often misread as an intensified ultimate, as if pen- meant 'beyond.' It actually means 'almost,' so the penultimate chapter is the one before the last, not a super-ultimate one. MW has a usage article specifically warning against the over-the-top reading.
exampleCorrect: The penultimate stage of the race climbs out of the valley before the riders coast into the city for the final sprint.
exampleMisused: Winning the championship in triple overtime was the penultimate moment of his entire career.

definition: By or in itself; considered on its own intrinsic nature apart from related factors or consequences. From the Latin meaning 'by itself.'
commonMisuse: Because it is heard more than read, per se is often miswritten as 'per say.' The phrase is Latin and ends in 'se'; there is no English word 'say' involved.
exampleCorrect: The job is not stressful per se, the lawyer explained, but the looming trial deadlines certainly are.
exampleMisused: In his memo he wrote that the policy was not unfair, per say, just poorly explained.

definition: Perpetuate means to keep something going, often indefinitely. Perpetrate, its near-twin, means to carry out or commit something, almost always something wrong or criminal. One keeps a thing echoing; the other is the original act.
commonMisuse: The words share a prefix and a rhythm, so writers swap them, saying a crook "perpetuated" a robbery or a stale joke "perpetrates" a stereotype. You perpetrate a crime once; you perpetuate a myth by helping it survive.
exampleCorrect: The crew that perpetrated the original fraud is gone, but every careless retelling perpetuates the myth that the bank was never to blame.
exampleMisused: The two suspects perpetuated the fraud over a single weekend before fleeing across the state line.

definition: A perquisite is a privilege or benefit attached to a job or position, beyond salary — the everyday short form is perk. A prerequisite is something required in advance before something else can happen.
commonMisuse: These near-identical words point opposite ways. A perquisite is something you get; a prerequisite is something you need. The corner office's view is a perquisite of the role; ten years of work is a prerequisite for it. The shared pre- syllable invites the slip.
exampleCorrect: Free parking in the underground garage was the one perquisite of the new job, though two years of fieldwork had been a prerequisite for the offer.
exampleMisused: Two years of fieldwork is a perquisite for the senior researcher role, no exceptions.

definition: Prospective is an adjective meaning likely to happen or potential, relating to the future. Perspective, often confused with it, is a noun meaning a particular way of looking at something — a point of view shaped by where you stand.
commonMisuse: The two share most of their letters but differ in job and meaning. Perspective is an angle of vision; prospective looks ahead in time. A prospective student tours the campus, then forms a perspective on it. Writers most often type prospective when they mean a fresh way of seeing.
exampleCorrect: From a prospective buyer's perspective, the cramped kitchen was the one flaw the glossy listing photos had carefully hidden.
exampleMisused: From her prospective, the merger looked like a disaster waiting to wreck both companies.

definition: Perspicuous describes writing or speech that is clear and easy to follow. Perspicacious, its near-twin, describes a person with sharp insight — quick to notice and understand.
commonMisuse: Reached for to sound erudite, the two get swapped because they look almost identical. The link to hold onto: a perspicacious mind produces perspicuous prose — the first is the seeing, the second is the clarity.
exampleCorrect: Her perspicacious reading of the raw data led to a perspicuous summary the whole board could follow.
exampleMisused: He admired the report's perspicacious wording, when he meant it was perspicuous — admirably clear.

definition: To read or examine something with close, careful attention, going over the material thoroughly rather than glancing at it briefly.
commonMisuse: Often used to mean skim or glance through, which is the opposite of the traditional sense. MW does list a 'read in a leisurely way' sense as accepted, but the word still implies attention, not speed. Context usually makes clear which sense is meant.
exampleCorrect: The auditor spent the entire afternoon perusing the ledger, flagging every irregularity she could find in the quarterly entries.
exampleMisused: I perused the menu real quick on the way in and just grabbed whatever the special was.

definition: A phenomenon is a single observable fact, event, or remarkable occurrence. Phenomena is the plural, referring to two or more of them.
commonMisuse: People say 'a phenomena' or 'this phenomena is fascinating', treating the plural as singular because it rolls off the tongue. The singular 'phenomenon' ends awkwardly in -non, so the smoother plural gets used for one thing by mistake.
exampleCorrect: The aurora is a stunning phenomenon, just one of many atmospheric phenomena that draw scientists to the far north each winter.
exampleMisused: Social media addiction is a troubling phenomena among teenagers today.

definition: To handle a situation as it unfolds, improvising and deciding what to do moment by moment rather than following a fixed plan or set of preparations made in advance.
commonMisuse: The idiom is borrowed from musicians who play a tune from memory without sheet music. People sometimes apply it too loosely to mean simply being lazy or unprepared, when it really describes flexible, responsive improvisation.
exampleCorrect: We had no itinerary for the weekend in Lisbon, so we decided to play it by ear and follow whatever looked interesting.
exampleMisused: Instead of studying for the interview, he shrugged and said he would just play it by ear and hope for the best.

definition: An excessive amount of something, more than is needed or healthy; in older medical use, an unhealthy oversupply of blood.
commonMisuse: Routinely used as a fancy way to say 'a lot,' which strips out the overshoot the word originally carried. MW now accepts the neutral 'abundance' sense as standard, but the older flavor of too much survives in careful writing: a plethora of options is usually a problem, not a feature.
exampleCorrect: The committee was paralyzed by a plethora of conflicting reports, none of which it had the time or expertise to fairly weigh.
exampleMisused: The new food hall offers a plethora of options, from Sichuan noodles to wood-fired pizza, all under one roof.

definition: A person of relentless, often naive optimism who insists on finding something good in every situation, sometimes to the point of ignoring real problems.
commonMisuse: It is sometimes used as a neutral compliment for a cheerful person. The term, from the heroine of Eleanor Porter's novel, usually carries a mild edge, suggesting optimism so unshakable it becomes unrealistic.
exampleCorrect: Ever the Pollyanna, she greeted the canceled flight by cheerfully declaring it a chance to explore the airport.
exampleMisused: He praised his new manager as a Pollyanna, meaning simply that she was friendly and supportive.

definition: To pore is to read or study something with sustained, intense concentration, almost always followed by over. To pour is to make a liquid flow in a stream from one container to another.
commonMisuse: The mix-up almost always runs one way: writers use pour over when they mean pore over. Studying a document carefully is poring over it; you only pour over something while holding a pitcher. Picture eyes traveling slowly down a page, not water spilling across a counter.
exampleCorrect: She pored over the old plot maps for hours while her husband poured coffee and tried not to interrupt.
exampleMisused: The analysts spent the weekend pouring over the leaked emails, looking for anything incriminating.

definition: To precede is to come before something else in time, order, or position. To proceed is to move forward, continue, or carry on with an action or process.
commonMisuse: Same Latin root, different prefixes, opposite directions. To precede is to go before; to proceed is to go forward. An opening act precedes the headliner, and once it ends the concert can proceed. The similar shapes make writers reach for the wrong one in either direction.
exampleCorrect: A short film about the playwright's early years preceded the main production, after which the audience proceeded quietly to their seats.
exampleMisused: Once the credits ended, the audience preceded straight to the parking garage.

definition: Precipitate, as a verb, means to cause something to happen suddenly; as an adjective, it means acting rashly without enough thought. Precipitous means dangerously steep, literally or figuratively.
commonMisuse: These cousins both come from the Latin for headlong, but precipitate is about haste while precipitous is about steepness. A precipitate decision is rash; a precipitous decline is a sharp fall. Writers often attach precipitous to hasty actions, or precipitate to cliffs.
exampleCorrect: The board's precipitate vote precipitated a precipitous drop in the foundation's stock, all within a single trading afternoon.
exampleMisused: The hike ended near a precipitate cliff that dropped almost a thousand feet.

definition: A premiere, with the final E, is the first public showing of a film, play, or show. Premier, without the E, means first in rank or quality, and as a noun is a head of government.
commonMisuse: People add the E to the ranking word ('a premiere athlete') because the showbiz spelling looks more polished. Reserve 'premiere' for opening nights. 'Premier' without the E means best, foremost, or a prime minister.
exampleCorrect: The director, the country's premier filmmaker, walked the red carpet at the premiere of her long-delayed feature.
exampleMisused: The resort bills itself as the premiere destination for winter skiing.

definition: To proscribe is to prohibit, condemn, or formally forbid something. To prescribe, its near-opposite, is to lay down as a rule or, in medicine, to order the use of a drug or treatment.
commonMisuse: These two sound nearly identical but mean almost opposites. Prescribe says do this; proscribe says don't. A doctor who proscribes a drug is forbidding it, which is rarely what the writer meant. The one-letter, one-prefix gap masks the total reversal of meaning.
exampleCorrect: Her doctor prescribed a low-dose medication, but the clinic's charter proscribed promoting any brand the staff held stock in.
exampleMisused: The new policy prescribes any discussion of last quarter's losses during the investor call.

definition: Traditionally, in a short while or soon; in modern usage, now or at the current moment, the sense that has become dominant.
commonMisuse: Once strictly meant soon, but the 'currently' sense has been widespread for so long that MW lists both as standard. The ambiguity is real: 'the manager is presently with a client' can be read as either right now or in a moment. Careful writers pick a clearer word.
exampleCorrect: Finish your tea, the carriage will be at the door presently, and the roads after dark are nothing to argue with.
exampleMisused: The CFO is presently reviewing the figures and should have a recommendation ready before the board convenes.

definition: To prevaricate is to stray from the truth, speaking evasively or misleadingly. To procrastinate is to delay or postpone something on purpose, usually out of reluctance or distraction.
commonMisuse: Both are long Latinate pr- verbs, which is most of why they get swapped, but one is about lying and the other about delay. A prevaricator deceives; a procrastinator simply puts things off. Saying he prevaricated on the deadline confuses dishonesty with foot-dragging.
exampleCorrect: She procrastinated on the report for weeks, then prevaricated to her boss about why it still wasn't finished.
exampleMisused: She kept prevaricating about scheduling the dentist appointment, putting it off week after week.

definition: Principal means main or most important, or names a person or sum — the head of a school, the lead in a deal, the original amount of a loan. Principle is always a noun: a fundamental rule or belief.
commonMisuse: Principle is always an idea; principal points to a person or amount, or means chief. If you can swap in idea, you want principle; if not, principal. A handy mnemonic: the principal of your school is your pal. The phrase on principle is the one most often misspelled.
exampleCorrect: Her principal objection was a matter of principle: she refused, on principle, to sign anything she could not read in full.
exampleMisused: The high school principle sent a long email home about the new phone policy.

definition: Supine means lying on the back, face up; figuratively it can also mean passive or unresisting. Prone, its opposite, means lying face down, with the front of the body toward the ground.
commonMisuse: In medical, yoga, and forensic contexts the difference is the whole point: prone is on the stomach, supine is on the back. Writers who only half-remember the pair often use prone for someone sprawled face up. A patient supine on the table is staring at the ceiling, not the floor.
exampleCorrect: The therapist had her lie prone for the back massage, then turn supine so he could examine her shoulder.
exampleMisused: He fell asleep on the couch in a prone position, staring up at the ceiling fan.

definition: The way a word is spoken, or the act of producing the sounds of speech.
commonMisuse: People spell it 'pronounciation', importing the O from the verb 'pronounce'. The noun drops that O: it is pro-nun-ci-a-tion. The verb keeps the 'noun' sound, but the noun shifts to 'nun', which trips up even confident spellers.
exampleCorrect: Her French was fluent, but a slight flaw in her pronunciation of one vowel still marked her as a non-native speaker.
exampleMisused: The teacher corrected my pronounciation of the new vocabulary words.

definition: Prophesy, a verb, is the act of predicting or foretelling. Prophecy, a noun, is the prediction itself — what is foretold. The s form is the doing; the c form is the thing.
commonMisuse: One letter and one part of speech apart, the two are constantly swapped. The pronunciation differs as a clue: prophecy ends in see, prophesy ends in sigh. Prophets prophesy a prophecy. Writers most often use prophecy where the verb prophesy belongs.
exampleCorrect: The temple oracle would prophesy each spring, and one ancient prophecy still hung, unfulfilled, over the harvest.
exampleMisused: She would often prophecy that the company's expansion plans would end in tears.

definition: Prostrate, with an extra R, means lying face down, or physically or emotionally overcome and helpless. The prostate, its near-twin, is a gland in the male reproductive system.
commonMisuse: People insert an extra R and say 'prostrate cancer' or 'prostrate exam'. The gland is prostate, with no R after the T. Conversely, someone overwhelmed is prostrate, lying flat, not 'prostate' with grief.
exampleCorrect: After his prostate screening came back clear, he was so relieved he nearly collapsed, prostrate with gratitude, onto the waiting room couch.
exampleMisused: His father is scheduled for a prostrate exam next week.

definition: A success won at a cost so ruinous to the victor that the win is scarcely worthwhile, leaving the winner nearly as damaged as the defeated side.
commonMisuse: People often apply it to any hard-fought or narrow win. Properly, a Pyrrhic victory is one whose losses are so devastating that it amounts almost to a defeat, named for King Pyrrhus's crippling battlefield triumphs.
exampleCorrect: The startup won the patent lawsuit, but the legal fees bankrupted it, making the verdict a Pyrrhic victory.
exampleMisused: After a tense final set, she called her three-hour tennis win a Pyrrhic victory because she was tired.

definition: In physics, the smallest discrete amount of a physical quantity that can exist, such as the minimum unit of energy involved in an interaction.
commonMisuse: In phrases like 'quantum leap' it is popularly used to mean a huge advance. Ironically, in physics a quantum is the smallest possible unit, so a quantum leap is literally a tiny jump.
exampleCorrect: An electron absorbs a quantum of energy and jumps to a higher orbital, the physicist explained at the chalkboard.
exampleMisused: The CEO promised the new phone was a quantum leap forward, a giant advance over last year's model.

definition: Representing the purest, most typical example of a class or quality; embodying the essential character of something better than any other instance.
commonMisuse: Sometimes used to mean 'most extreme' or 'ultimate,' as if it were a fancier version of ultimate. The real sense is closer to 'most typical' or 'most representative.' A quintessential New York deli is the one that captures what a deli is, not the biggest or fanciest one.
exampleCorrect: With its tin ceiling and counter seats, the diner is the quintessential roadside stop, almost a museum piece of a vanishing kind.
exampleMisused: Climbing the north face in winter without ropes is the quintessential test of how far an alpinist will push themselves.

definition: Marked by lofty but unrealistic idealism, pursuing noble visions in a way that is impractical, romantic, and likely to end in failure despite sincere good intentions.
commonMisuse: It is sometimes used loosely to mean merely whimsical or unpredictable. Properly, quixotic, from Cervantes's Don Quixote, describes idealism so impractical that it ignores reality in chasing grand ideals.
exampleCorrect: His quixotic plan to end city poverty single-handedly with one charity bake sale was doomed but heartfelt.
exampleMisused: The designer's quixotic taste in colors made the office feel cheerfully unpredictable.

definition: To strain mentally and search hard through one's memory or thoughts. The image is the rack, a medieval torture device that stretched the body, so the mind is being painfully stretched for an answer.
commonMisuse: Many people write "wrack one's brain." "Wrack" is a genuine but rare word tied to "wreck" and ruin, and because thinking hard feels destructive, writers reach for it. Most usage guides treat "rack" as the standard, correct form.
exampleCorrect: I racked my brain for the name of the hotel where we stayed in Lisbon, but it simply would not come back to me.
exampleMisused: She wracked her brain trying to remember where she'd left the spare key.

definition: To ravish is to carry off by force or, more commonly today, to fill someone with intense delight or rapture. To ravage, its near-twin, is to inflict severe, sustained destruction, leaving something laid waste.
commonMisuse: They share a Latin root but pull opposite ways: ravage is destructive, ravish (in modern use) is enchanting. A drought ravages the countryside; a symphony ravishes its listeners. Confusing them can produce unintentionally lurid sentences about hurricanes ravishing the coast.
exampleCorrect: Wildfires continued to ravage the canyon, even as the sunsets they produced ravished every photographer on the ridge.
exampleMisused: Hurricane winds threatened to ravish the coastline, the anchor reported, when he meant ravage.

definition: To raze is to tear something down to the ground, leveling it completely — the verb of demolition crews and bulldozers. To raise is the opposite: to lift up, build, or elevate.
commonMisuse: Raze and raise are pronounced identically, so the confusion is usually visual — a reader meets the unfamiliar raze in print and assumes a typo. But the z reverses everything: you raze a building to flatten it, you raise one to construct it.
exampleCorrect: The city voted to raze the abandoned mill on the riverbank and raise a public library in its place.
exampleMisused: Developers plan to raise the old warehouse next month, the article said, when it was scheduled to be razed.

definition: Something introduced into a discussion or story to pull attention away from what actually matters. The name comes from the strong-smelling smoked fish supposedly used to throw hunting dogs off a scent.
commonMisuse: Red herring isn't just any false lead; it's a distraction, usually a deliberate one, from the real issue. People sometimes use it for anything that turns out to be wrong, but a guess that fails isn't a red herring. A red herring is laid down to mislead.
exampleCorrect: The missing watch turned out to be a red herring, planted by the killer to send the inspector chasing the wrong suspect.
exampleMisused: She called the broken streetlight a red herring in the case, though no one had been trying to draw attention to it.

definition: Phrases like PIN number, ATM machine, HIV virus, ISBN number, and LCD display repeat a word already inside the acronym, since the final letter already stands for number, machine, virus, or display. True fact and tuna fish are similar redundancies.
commonMisuse: Because most speakers don't mentally unpack an acronym, they tack on a clarifying noun the abbreviation already contains — PIN number expands to personal identification number number. It feels natural and harmless in speech, but careful writing trims the extra word.
exampleCorrect: At the checkout she entered her PIN, then walked to the ATM in the lobby to withdraw cash for the weekend.
exampleMisused: He stood at the ATM machine muttering that he had forgotten his PIN number again.

definition: To rebut a claim is to argue against it or offer an opposing case, without necessarily disproving it. To refute a claim, by contrast, is to prove it false or wrong with evidence and argument.
commonMisuse: People use 'refute' to mean merely deny or contest: 'the senator refuted the allegations'. But refuting requires actually proving the point false. If someone simply pushes back without proof, they have rebutted or denied, not refuted.
exampleCorrect: The lab results refuted the rumor outright, though the spokesperson had earlier tried only to rebut it with carefully worded statements.
exampleMisused: The actor refuted the tabloid story by saying it was completely untrue.

definition: Regretful describes a person who feels sorrow over something done or undone — an internal emotional state. Regrettable describes a situation, outcome, or action unfortunate enough to deserve being regretted.
commonMisuse: People are regretful; circumstances are regrettable. Writers often reach for regretful when they mean regrettable, attaching the feeling to a thing that cannot feel anything — calling a vote a regretful decision when the voters felt no remorse.
exampleCorrect: She sounded genuinely regretful on the voicemail, calling the harsh words she had thrown across the kitchen a regrettable mistake.
exampleMisused: The newspaper called the council's vote a regretful decision, though the council itself felt no remorse.

definition: A rein is the leather strap used to guide and check a horse, and by extension any means of control or restraint. A reign is the period or act of a monarch's rule, or any dominant tenure.
commonMisuse: You rein in a horse or a budget; a monarch reigns over a country. The g in reign matches regal, sovereign, and government — all words about ruling. The classic slip is free reign, which should always be free rein, a horse given slack in the straps.
exampleCorrect: During the queen's long reign, her stewards kept a tight rein on the household budget right down to the candle wax.
exampleMisused: The new CEO gave her marketing team free reign to redesign the brand.

definition: Stubbornly resisting control or direction, like a horse that refuses to move forward. By extension, also marked by impatience or uneasiness in a person or crowd.
commonMisuse: The trickier confusion is internal to the word itself. The original sense was 'balky' — refusing to budge — but modern usage has drifted toward 'fidgety' or 'restless,' nearly the opposite. Both senses now appear in dictionaries, but the older meaning can catch readers off guard.
exampleCorrect: The mare grew restive at the starting gate, planting her hooves and refusing every coaxing word from the jockey.
exampleMisused: The audience grew restive during the third hour, shifting in their seats and checking their phones impatiently.

definition: Reticent means reserved, quiet, or unwilling to speak freely. Reluctant means unwilling or hesitant to do something of any kind.
commonMisuse: People use 'reticent' as a fancier word for reluctant: 'reticent to sign the lease' or 'reticent to travel'. Strictly, reticence is about holding back speech. If no silence or reserve is involved, 'reluctant' is the word you want.
exampleCorrect: Normally reticent about his private life, the author was nonetheless reluctant to cancel the interview and disappoint his readers.
exampleMisused: She was reticent to lend him the car after his last accident.

definition: Full of or abounding in something, almost always something unwelcome such as errors, rumors, or disease. "Rife" means widespread, prevalent, or occurring in great numbers.
commonMisuse: People write "ripe with," since "ripe" is a common word and "rife" is not, and the two sound very close. "Ripe" suggests something full and ready, so the swap feels meaningful even though it loses the negative sense "rife" carries.
exampleCorrect: The early draft of the contract was rife with ambiguities that the company's lawyers spent a week untangling.
exampleMisused: The investigation found the agency was ripe with corruption and mismanagement.

definition: A ceremony, event, or experience that formally marks an important transition in someone's life, such as moving from childhood into adulthood or from one social status to another.
commonMisuse: It is frequently miswritten as 'right of passage,' as though it described a legal entitlement to travel through somewhere. The correct word is 'rite,' meaning a ritual or ceremony, which has nothing to do with permission or rights.
exampleCorrect: For the village teenagers, the solo overnight hike into the mountains was a long-standing rite of passage into adulthood.
exampleMisused: She wrote in her essay that getting a first apartment was an important right of passage for any young person.

definition: Burglary is unlawfully entering a building intending to commit a crime inside, whether or not anyone is present. Robbery, often confused with it, is unlawfully taking property directly from a person by force or threat of violence, so confrontation is essential.
commonMisuse: People use the two interchangeably, but the legal line is sharp. Robbery requires a victim confronted with force; an empty house cannot be robbed, only burglarized. Coming home to a ransacked house is a burglary, even though everyday speech calls it being robbed.
exampleCorrect: The masked man committed robbery at the teller's window downtown, then drove home to a house that had suffered a burglary while he was out.
exampleMisused: She reported a robbery after returning from vacation to find her empty home broken into and ransacked.

definition: Existing in only a basic, undeveloped, or incomplete form; belonging to an early or primitive stage of something's development.
commonMisuse: Reached for as an impressive synonym for 'fundamental' or 'essential' — 'a rudimentary principle of the theory.' Merriam-Webster does list 'fundamental' as a sense, but in practice the word almost always signals that something is minimal, crude, or only half-formed. Calling your grasp of a subject 'rudimentary' says you barely know it, not that you command the basics.
exampleCorrect: After a month in Lisbon he had only a rudimentary command of Portuguese — enough to order dinner, but not to follow the news.
exampleMisused: Decades of settled law rest on this rudimentary principle of fairness — the bedrock the whole system is built on.

definition: A person or group unfairly blamed for the mistakes or wrongdoing of others. It comes from a biblical ritual in which a goat symbolically carried away the community's sins into the wilderness.
commonMisuse: Many people hear "escape goat," which seems to make sense: the blamed party lets the real culprits escape consequences. But the word is "scape," an old shortening of "escape," fused to "goat." The modern ear simply restores the missing syllable it expects to hear.
exampleCorrect: When the project collapsed, management made the junior analyst the scapegoat rather than admitting their own budgeting failures.
exampleMisused: They needed an escape goat, so they blamed the new guy for the whole mess.

definition: A well-tested explanatory framework supported by extensive evidence, used to make predictions and organize knowledge. It is not a hunch or guess but a structure that has survived repeated attempts to disprove it.
commonMisuse: In everyday speech a theory is closer to a guess: I have a theory about why he was late. In science the word means something stronger, on the order of evolution by natural selection or general relativity. Dismissing one of those as just a theory mixes the two senses.
exampleCorrect: Plate tectonics is a scientific theory backed by decades of evidence from earthquakes, ocean trenches, and fossil records on separated continents.
exampleMisused: He waved off the climate data, saying global warming was just a scientific theory and therefore not really settled.

definition: Completely free from punishment, obligation, or harm. The 'scot' was a medieval tax or assessment, so to go scot-free originally meant to escape one's share of the bill.
commonMisuse: Scot-free sometimes appears as 'scott-free,' as though named after someone called Scott. The 'scot' here has nothing to do with Scotland or anyone's first name; it refers to a tax that medieval townspeople owed. Going scot-free meant slipping out without paying it.
exampleCorrect: Two of the defendants accepted plea deals, but the ringleader somehow walked away scot-free.
exampleMisused: He got off scott-free after the audit, the column reported, when scot-free was the proper spelling.

definition: A smooth, uninterrupted transition from one thing to another, especially in conversation, music, or film. Pronounced SEG-way, it comes from Italian for 'follows'.
commonMisuse: People spell it 'segway', borrowing the look of the two-wheeled Segway scooter, whose brand name was deliberately built on the word. The pronunciation is right but the spelling is wrong, and writing 'segway' in an email reverses the joke entirely.
exampleCorrect: She handled the awkward topic change gracefully, using a quick anecdote about traffic as a segue into the budget discussion nobody wanted.
exampleMisused: And that's a nice segway into our next slide about the quarterly numbers.

definition: Sensuous appeals to the senses through beauty, art, or fine experience, without bodily implication. Sensual, often used in its place, relates to gratification of the physical, bodily senses, often with sexual or indulgent connotations.
commonMisuse: Milton coined sensuous precisely to discuss the senses without the sexual baggage of sensual. Many writers now use them interchangeably, but calling a cathedral's stained glass or a violin solo sensual instead of sensuous can carry an unintended innuendo.
exampleCorrect: Critics praised the painter's sensuous use of light, while noting the sensual heat of the bathhouse scenes she lingered over.
exampleMisused: The brochure described the cathedral's stained glass as sensual, when sensuous would have avoided the innuendo.

definition: To enter or arrive at a place, often used to mark a notable first or last visit. You can set foot in a country for the first time or vow never to set foot in a restaurant again.
commonMisuse: Many writers say or type 'step foot' instead of set foot. Traditional usage favors set foot, but step foot has been around for centuries and now appears in Merriam-Webster's example sentences without a usage warning. Both forms are widely heard, though set foot remains the more accepted choice in formal writing.
exampleCorrect: She had not set foot in the old farmhouse since her mother's funeral nearly fifteen years earlier.
exampleMisused: I'll never step foot in that diner again, he muttered, using the variant that some readers will still flag.

definition: A frozen dessert made from fruit juice or puree with a little milk or cream, lighter than ice cream and tangier than sorbet. It is spelled and said with one r: SHER-bet.
commonMisuse: People say and write "sherbert," inserting a second r. The extra r mirrors the rhythm of words like "Herbert" and feels balanced to the mouth, so the doubled sound spreads even though only the first r belongs.
exampleCorrect: On the hottest afternoon of August, the diner served scoops of rainbow sherbet in chilled metal bowls.
exampleMisused: Could I get a scoop of orange sherbert in a waffle cone?

definition: A word, phrase, or custom that distinguishes members of a group from outsiders. The term comes from a Biblical episode where the pronunciation of one Hebrew word betrayed enemy soldiers trying to pass undetected.
commonMisuse: Shibboleth has a second, related sense: an empty slogan or worn-out doctrine repeated more from habit than conviction. The two meanings sit close together, so writers sometimes use shibboleth simply to mean cliche, missing the original idea of a marker that identifies who belongs.
exampleCorrect: Pronouncing the town's name correctly was a small shibboleth, instantly marking locals apart from weekend visitors.
exampleMisused: He dismissed her advice as the same old shibboleth, meaning only that he had heard it many times before.

definition: Someone or something certain to win, with the implication that the victory will come easily. The term comes from horse racing slang for a horse 'shooed' across the finish line in a fixed race.
commonMisuse: Shoo-in often gets written as 'shoe-in,' which suggests squeezing a foot into a tight loafer. The actual image is shooing — waving an arm to drive a chicken, or a horse, in a particular direction. There is no shoe involved, however much the spelling 'shoe-in' might tempt the eye.
exampleCorrect: After her semifinal sweep, she was considered a shoo-in for the gold medal, which is exactly what she took home.
exampleMisused: He's a shoe-in for the promotion, everyone says so, the office gossip ran, where shoo-in was needed.

definition: Brief and dismissive treatment; little time, attention, or sympathy given to someone or something. "Shrift" was the short confession and absolution once granted to a condemned prisoner before execution.
commonMisuse: People write "short shift," thinking of a brief work stint. "Shrift" is essentially obsolete outside this phrase, and "shift" is one consonant away and instantly meaningful, so it quietly takes over the idiom.
exampleCorrect: The committee gave the neighborhood's flooding concerns short shrift, spending barely two minutes on them before adjourning.
exampleMisused: The report on safety issues got short shift at yesterday's meeting.

definition: Treating something as simpler than it actually is, to the point of leaving out important nuance; oversimplified in a misleading way.
commonMisuse: Often used as a neutral synonym for simple, but simplistic is reliably pejorative. To call an explanation simplistic is to say it has flattened a complex problem until it no longer reflects reality. Simple can be a virtue; simplistic almost never is.
exampleCorrect: His simplistic account of the crash blamed a single trader and ignored the years of regulatory drift that made the collapse inevitable.
exampleMisused: I love the simplistic design of the new kitchen, just white tile and oak shelves and nothing else cluttering the walls.

definition: Describing labor that is endless, repetitive, and ultimately futile, demanding continual effort that never reaches completion because progress is always undone.
commonMisuse: People often use it simply to mean 'very hard.' Properly, Sisyphean refers to the myth of Sisyphus endlessly rolling a boulder uphill only to watch it roll back, so the key idea is futility, not mere difficulty.
exampleCorrect: Clearing the inbox felt Sisyphean: for every message she answered, three new ones immediately appeared.
exampleMisused: He described the steep uphill bike ride as Sisyphean, though he reached the summit and was done.

definition: Skillful manual trickery, especially the deft, deceptive movements a magician uses; by extension, any cunning deception or clever misdirection. "Sleight" is an old word meaning cunning, dexterity, or craft.
commonMisuse: People routinely write "slight of hand," because "slight" is a familiar everyday word while "sleight" is nearly extinct outside this phrase. The two are pronounced identically, so the mistake never surfaces in speech and only appears, jarringly, in writing.
exampleCorrect: Through sheer sleight of hand, the street magician made the borrowed wedding ring vanish and reappear inside a sealed lemon.
exampleMisused: The accountant moved the funds with a quick slight of hand nobody noticed.

definition: A quick, often unofficial advance look at something not yet released or revealed, such as a film, product, or document. "Peek" means a brief or furtive glance.
commonMisuse: Writers constantly type "sneak peak," swapping the homophone for a mountain summit. "Peek" and "peak" sound identical, and "peak" is the more common spelling, so autopilot and spellcheck both happily wave the wrong word through.
exampleCorrect: The studio released a thirty-second sneak peek of the finale, just enough footage to ignite fan theories online.
exampleMisused: Here is a sneak peak at our new spring collection, available next week.

definition: Marked by strict self-discipline, austerity, and the deliberate absence of comfort or luxury, in the manner attributed to the rigorously trained citizens of ancient Sparta.
commonMisuse: It is sometimes used loosely to mean simply minimal or sparsely decorated. The word properly carries a sense of disciplined hardship and endurance, not just an uncluttered aesthetic.
exampleCorrect: The monks lived in spartan cells with a straw mat, a single candle, and no heat through winter.
exampleMisused: She called the chic, half-empty loft spartan because it had only one stylish sofa.

definition: Someone or something that looks strikingly like someone or something else, especially in family resemblance. A child can be the spitting image of a parent down to the slope of the chin.
commonMisuse: The phrase began life as 'spit and image' or 'spit-image,' suggesting someone so like the original they might have been spat out of them. 'Spitting image' is now the dominant form, and dictionaries treat it as standard. The older variants survive in some regional and literary writing.
exampleCorrect: Their youngest son is the spitting image of his grandfather, right down to the lopsided grin in the wedding photos.
exampleMisused: She was the spit and image of her great-aunt, the obituary said, using a much older form that few readers recognize today.

definition: A law that sets the maximum time after an event during which legal action can be taken. Once the statute expires, the offense usually cannot be prosecuted or sued over.
commonMisuse: In speech, statute of limitations often gets heard or typed as 'statue of limitations,' as if it referred to a piece of sculpture. Statute means a written law, while statue means a carved figure. The two words sound nearly identical, and the legal sense is unfamiliar enough that the swap is common.
exampleCorrect: The prosecutor explained that the statute of limitations for the fraud had run out exactly nine days before the warrant arrived.
exampleMisused: I think the statue of limitations protects him at this point, she said over coffee, where statute was the proper word.

definition: Excessively strict, proper, or morally rigid in attitude and behavior. "Strait" means narrow or tight, so the image is of a corset laced uncomfortably tight, allowing no slack.
commonMisuse: People write "straight-laced," assuming a connection to being upright or "straight" in conduct. "Strait" and "straight" are homophones, and "straight" is far more common, so the familiar spelling overwrites the older "strait" meaning narrow.
exampleCorrect: Her strait-laced uncle disapproved of the rehearsal dinner toast and left before the second round of jokes.
exampleMisused: My new boss is so straight-laced he won't even let us play music in the office.

definition: A weakened or distorted version of someone's argument, set up specifically because it's easier to knock down than the real one. The image is of attacking a scarecrow instead of a real opponent.
commonMisuse: Straw man is sometimes used for any argument you disagree with, which dilutes it. The fallacy specifically involves misrepresenting the other side's position. If you accurately restate their view and then refute it, that's just an argument; it only becomes a straw man when you've reshaped their claim first.
exampleCorrect: Her opponent built a straw man, pretending she had called for banning all cars rather than for expanding bike lanes.
exampleMisused: He dismissed every critique as a straw man, even the ones that quoted his own position back to him verbatim.

definition: According to what is said or believed, often with a hint of doubt. It marks a claim as second-hand information rather than something the speaker is willing to vouch for.
commonMisuse: Supposedly often gets pronounced or written as 'supposably.' Merriam-Webster actually recognizes 'supposably' as a real word meaning 'as may be supposed' — possible to suppose — which is subtly different from supposedly's 'as is supposed.' In practice almost everyone reaching for 'supposably' actually wants supposedly.
exampleCorrect: He supposedly trained with a Michelin chef in Lyon, though no one at the restaurant has ever confirmed the story.
exampleMisused: She's supposably moving to Berlin in the fall, he said, where supposedly was almost certainly the intended word.

definition: To assume something is true or will always be available without questioning it, often failing to appreciate its value. "Granted" here means accepted or conceded as a given.
commonMisuse: People write "take for granite," because "granted" is unstressed and swallowed in speech, while "granite" is a real, concrete word. The rock even suggests something solid and dependable, which makes the wrong version feel oddly logical.
exampleCorrect: Living a block from the ocean, we took the sunset walks for granted until we moved inland and missed them terribly.
exampleMisused: Don't take your health for granite; you never know what tomorrow brings.

definition: A statement that says the same thing twice in different words, or in logic, a statement that is true purely by its form. Free gift is a tautology; so is it will either rain or not rain tomorrow.
commonMisuse: Tautology gets confused with redundancy in casual use, and the two do overlap. In logic, though, a tautology has a precise sense: a statement true under every interpretation. The everyday meaning is looser, covering any phrase that doubles back on itself.
exampleCorrect: The phrase advance planning is a tautology, since planning by definition happens in advance of the thing planned.
exampleMisused: He accused her of tautology for repeating her main point, though restating an argument and committing one are not the same thing.

definition: A tenet is a principle, doctrine, or belief held by a person or group. A tenant, its near-twin, is a person who rents or occupies property.
commonMisuse: The words look and sound nearly alike, so people write 'a central tenant of the philosophy' when they mean tenet. The mix-up feels safe because 'tenant' is the far more common word, so it springs to mind first.
exampleCorrect: Nonviolence is a core tenet of the movement, one the new tenants of the community house pledged to uphold.
exampleMisused: Honesty is the main tenant of our company culture.

word: the exception that proves the rule
definition: An expression derived from a Latin legal maxim, originally meaning that the existence of a stated exception implies a general rule applying to all cases not specifically excepted.
commonMisuse: It is widely used to suggest, illogically, that an exception somehow confirms a rule it actually contradicts. The original sense uses 'prove' to mean 'test,' or notes that naming an exception implies an unstated rule.
exampleCorrect: A sign reading 'free parking on Sundays' implies paid parking on other days, the exception that proves the rule.
exampleMisused: He's always late, but he was punctual once, which I guess is the exception that proves the rule.

word: the proof of the pudding is in the eating
definition: The true worth or quality of something can only be judged by trying it out. Plans and promises mean little; the real test is whether the thing actually works when you use it.
commonMisuse: The proverb commonly gets shortened to 'the proof is in the pudding,' which has become so widespread that dictionaries now list it alongside the full version. The original makes more logical sense — the test is in the eating — but both forms are now widely accepted in standard usage.
exampleCorrect: The startup's pitch deck looked great, but the proof of the pudding is in the eating, and their first product launch flopped.
exampleMisused: The proof is in the pudding, the analyst declared, using the now-common short form rather than the original full proverb.

definition: Hard, painful struggles or violent spasms, often used figuratively for a difficult transition. Death throes, the throes of revolution, the throes of grief — all suggest intense, wrenching effort.
commonMisuse: Throes routinely gets typed as 'throws,' the more familiar word meaning to toss. They sound identical but mean entirely different things. The throes of grief are agonies; the throws of grief would be hurling objects across the room. The h before the o is the visual giveaway.
exampleCorrect: The newspaper was in the throes of its third layoff in a year, with whole departments emptying out by Friday.
exampleMisused: She was in the throws of a deadline panic, the post read, instead of the correct throes.

definition: Suited from birth or upbringing to a particular role, custom, or way of life. The phrase comes from Hamlet and originally describes someone bred to a tradition rather than someone born into wealth.
commonMisuse: Shakespeare's phrase 'to the manner born' (born to the custom) is often heard as 'to the manor born' (born to a grand estate). Merriam-Webster now lists 'to the manor born' as its own entry, accepting both forms. The manner version is the original; the manor version is a centuries-old pun that has become standard in its own right.
exampleCorrect: Watching her work the room at her first diplomatic reception, you'd have thought she was to the manner born.
exampleMisused: He carries himself like he was to the manor born, the columnist wrote, using the now-accepted variant with its aristocratic flavor.
